verses.text: If you have rashly pledged surety for another, the father urges, humble yourself and free yourself swiftly, like a roe from the hunter. Hasty financial bonds are a snare. Wisdom counsels caution in promises that can entrap us.

'Go to the ant, thou sluggard; consider her ways, and be wise,' for she gathers her food in season without any overseer. Even an insect rebukes laziness. The diligent provide in good time, needing no one to drive them.

'Go to the ant, thou sluggard; consider her ways, and be wise,' for she gathers her food in season without any overseer. Even an insect rebukes laziness. The diligent provide in good time, needing no one to drive them.

'How long wilt thou sleep, O sluggard?' for a little folding of the hands, and poverty comes 'as one that travelleth.' Idleness invites ruin. The little indulgences of sloth add up to want that arrives like an armed man.

A worthless man walks with a froward mouth, winking and plotting mischief; 'therefore shall his calamity come suddenly.' Deceit ripens into disaster. The schemer's ruin overtakes him in a moment, beyond remedy.

'These six things doth the LORD hate,' even seven: a proud look, lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood, a wicked heart, swift feet to evil, false witness, and one who sows discord. God hates these sins intensely. What He abhors, His people must put far away.

Keep thy father's commandment and mother's law, binding them on thy heart, 'for the commandment is a lamp; and the law is light.' God's word guides and warns. The instruction received in youth lights the way through life.

It keeps a man from the evil woman and the flattery of the harlot, who reduces him 'to a piece of bread' and hunts the precious life. Lust impoverishes and destroys. The fleeting pleasure costs a man everything worth keeping.

'Can a man take fire in his bosom, and his clothes not be burned?' So no one who touches his neighbour's wife goes unpunished. Sin has unavoidable consequences. To play with adultery is to be certainly scorched by it.

'Can a man take fire in his bosom, and his clothes not be burned?' So no one who touches his neighbour's wife goes unpunished. Sin has unavoidable consequences. To play with adultery is to be certainly scorched by it.

A starving thief may be pitied yet must restore sevenfold; but the adulterer 'destroyeth his own soul,' earning wounds and a reproach not wiped away. His sin is graver still. Adultery brings ruin that no restitution can undo.

For 'jealousy is the rage of a man,' who will accept no ransom or gift to be appeased. The wronged husband cannot be bought off. The adulterer faces a fury that no payment can satisfy.
